docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
This documentation will guide you through how to host a website through the use of Node.js and Express.js. Our goal is to provide a framework that is reusable and can be modified in the future for different websites. Our documentation will guide you through the basics.

  • Node.js is a cross-platform, open-source runtime environment that runs the V8 JavaScript engine outside of a web browser
  • Express.js is web application framework, built on the top of node js, that provides features for building web applications. It helps manage routes and servers

docs.title: Intended Users
docs.text: This documentation is designed for users who are beginners with web development and need to set up their own website.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
This documentation is designed to be used with the following assumptions:

  • User has the latest version of vsCode installed.
  • User is using a Windows Operating System.

docs.title: Task Overview
docs.text:
Here are the following tasks that our guide will help walk you through:

  • Getting Started with Express JS
  • Connecting to GitHub and Installing Nodemon
  • Using Get/Send/Listen
  • Connecting a website using Express

docs.title: Conventions
docs.text:
• Images will be annotated with red boxes to highlight key information
  • All steps will be numbered sequentially
  • Images with blocks of code will have an option, in the top right corner of the image, to copy the code in that image which provides a shortcut to copy and paste as shown below: 
    This text can be copied with the icon in the top right corner.

docs.title: Messages and Notes
docs.text:
Warning

Messages that can lead to potential errors will be specified with this tag.

Info

Messages that contain helpful information will be specified with this tag.

Success

Messages that lead to a sucessful completion of a task will be specified with this tag.

docs.title: JavaScript (JS)
docs.text: A language used to make websites interactive, like adding buttons that do something when clicked.

docs.title: Node.js
docs.text: A program that allows JavaScript to run on a computer, not just inside a website.

docs.title: ExpressJS
docs.text: A tool that helps build websites and apps using Node.js, making it easier to manage how they work.

docs.title: Terminal
docs.text: A screen where you type commands to control your computer. It’s available in most coding programs (IDEs).

docs.title: GitHub
docs.text: A website where people store and share code, making it easy to work on projects with others.

docs.title: Nodemon
docs.text: A tool that watches your project and restarts it if something goes wrong, so you don’t have to do it manually.

docs.title: npm
docs.text: A tool that helps you easily install and manage extra features for your Node.js project.

docs.title: Boilerplate
docs.text: A starter set of code that helps you begin a new project without writing everything from scratch.

docs.title: Localhost
docs.text: A special web address that points to your own computer, often used for testing projects before they go online.

docs.title: GET
docs.text: A way for a website or app to ask a server for information, like loading a webpage or fetching data.

docs.title: Listen
docs.text: A command that tells a server to wait and be ready for people to connect to it.

docs.title: Send
docs.text: A command that delivers information from a server to a user, like sending a webpage or a message.

docs.title: HTML
docs.text: The basic building blocks of a webpage, used to create and organize content.

docs.title: Extension
docs.text: An extra feature you can add to a program or browser to give it more abilities.

docs.title: JSON
docs.text: A simple way to store and share information, often used to send data between websites and apps.

docs.title: Getting Started with Express JS
docs.text: In this section, we will go over the steps to installing and creating our first file.

docs.title: Creating our file
docs.text:
1 Select new file that you will be creating

2 Name our file to whatever name of your choice, make sure to include .js at the end

docs.title: Getting Started with Express JS
docs.text:
Next we will install Express from the terminal

3 Navigate to the terminal window by going to VIEW --> TERMINAL

Info

This can also be done with the shortcut: _(CTRL + SHIFT + `)

4 Once the terminal has opened, click into it and typee the following into the terminal: npm install express

npm install express

docs.title: Copy over the boilerplate code
docs.text:
5 We will next copy over the boilerplate code provided to us by Express

const express = require('express')
const app = express()
const port = 3000

app.get('/', (req, res) => {
  res.send('Hello World!')
})

app.listen(port, () => {
  console.log(`Example app listening on port ${port}`)
})

Info

This code can also be found on the Express JS Setting Started website

docs.title: Getting started with npm
docs.text:
6 Navigate to the terminal window by going to VIEW --> TERMINAL

Info

This can also be done with the shortcut: (CTRL + SHIFT + `)

7 Once the terminal has opened, click into it and type the following: npm init 

npm init

Info

After we have typed npm init, we will be asked to enter details about our file

8 Continuously press ENTER until all done

docs.title: Checking our steps
docs.text:
9 Navigate to the newly created package.json that npm has created for us

10 Verify the names inside package.json match what we have installed

11 To verify we did the last steps correctly, type the following into the terminal: node .\filename

node .\filename

12 Finally add a .gitignore file to our project, this will allow us to upload our file to github

Success

Your page should look like this:

docs.title: Connecting to Github and Installing Nodemon
docs.text: The following steps will explain how to connect our newly created file to your github, as well as install nodemon. Nodemon will allow us to view our file and have it auto update, which will aleviate unecessary repitition.

docs.title: Github
docs.text:
1 Intiliaze github in VSCode 2 Check our file by typing the following in the terminal: node .\filename

node .\filename

3 Add the names of the files we created in the previous step to .gitignore (node_module/*)

docs.title: Installing Nodemon
docs.text:
4 nagivate to the terminal as we did in our last step (VIEW --> TERMINAL) and type the following: npm install -g nodemon

npm install -g nodemon

5 We will start Nodemon by typing the following in the terminal: nodemon .\filename.js

nodemon .\filename.js

docs.title: Testing our file with Nodemon
docs.text:
6 Open up a browser and type the following: localhost:3000/

localhost:3000/

Success

Your page should look like this:

7 Return back to the file and modify hello world response 8 Change the port number from 3000 to another number from 1-64536

docs.title: Using Get\Send\Listen
docs.text: In the next steps, we will go over how to start using get, send and listen in Express JS.

docs.title: Use the Get Request Boilerplate
docs.text:
1 Copy the Get request provided and paste it into your file

const express = require('express')
const app = express()
const port = 3000

app.get('/', (req, res) => {
res.send('Hello World!')
})

app.listen(port, () => {
console.log(`Example app listening on port ${port}`)
})

Info

The boilerplate code can also be found at this website

docs.title: Getting started with res.send
docs.text:
• Next, we will explain how res.send will send a message 2 Type the following in the terminal: node .\filename
node .\filename

Info

In the same terminal, the program can be stopped by typing the following: CTRL + UPPERCASE C_

3 Copy the following code into your vsCode, which will send our first message

app.get("/", (req, res) => {
    res.send("<h1> Welcome to my first site!</h1>")
})

docs.title: Testing our app.listen
docs.text:
• Now we wil text our app.listen message to view what we just created with res.send

4 open up a browser of your choice and type: localhost:3000

localhost:3000

5 Check our generic message

Success

Your page should look like this:

docs.title: Using res.send for a new page
docs.text:
6 Now we will go back to vsCode to send a message to a new page. Copy the following code:

app.get("/page1", (req, res) => {
    res.send("<h1> Welcome to page1!</h1>")
})

7 Lets test this page by the /page route and check our result

Success

Your page should look like this:

docs.title: Connecting a website using Express
docs.text: In these steps, we will finally connect our web page using express.

docs.title: Creating a template HTML page
docs.text:
1 Create a new html file

  • Name the file: home.html

2 Once the file is created, click into it

Info

At the top of the page, we will be utilizing a shortcut to make a boilerplate html page

3 Copy the following text and paste it into your new file

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8">
    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
    <title>Document</title>
</head>
<body>

</body>
</html>

4 Add a message inside the

5 Lets verify that our html page is working by checking our localhost:3000!

Success

Your page should look like this:

docs.title: Connecting our website through express
docs.text:
6 Go back to our other file where the .send is

  • copy the destination path to the html page we just created
  • Update our app.get

docs.title: Testing our new website with app.get
docs.text:
Info

With our new get request, we can now test our new website!

7 lets add a messgae to our json object

8 navigate to localhost:3000 and you should see the new addition

Success

Your page should look like this:

docs.title: Installing json viewer and testing our new website
docs.text:
9 go to extensions page

  • search for json viewer
  • download extension
  • use extension to test our website

10 selecting the parse option will allow us to see our json object in a user friendly way

Success

Your page should look like this:
